questions.question_text: Hawaii's mandatory seller disclosure law, HRS Chapter 508D, applies to which type of residential property?
questions.explanation: HRS Chapter 508D covers residential real property with 1 to 4 dwelling units, including residential condominiums and co-ops. Commercial properties and larger multifamily buildings are not covered.

questions.question_text: Under HRS 508D, a seller must disclose material facts. What standard determines whether a fact is 'material'?
questions.correct_answer: It would measurably affect value to a reasonable person
distractors: It involves any property defect over $500
It was discovered within the last 5 years
It relates only to structural components
questions.explanation: A material fact is any fact that would measurably affect the value of the property to a reasonable person. This broad standard covers structural defects, environmental hazards, zoning issues, and more.

questions.question_text: Which disclosure form must a Hawaii seller complete to satisfy the requirements of HRS Chapter 508D?
questions.explanation: The SRPDS (Seller's Real Property Disclosure Statement) is the required form under HRS 508D. It covers structural defects, systems, pest damage, water intrusion, zoning, and environmental hazards.

questions.question_text: A seller is transferring a condo unit that was part of a developer's public report filing. Is the seller required to provide the SRPDS?
questions.correct_answer: No, this transfer is exempt from 508D
distractors: Yes, all condo sales require the SRPDS
Only if the unit has been modified
Only if the buyer requests it in writing
questions.explanation: HRS 508D exempts several transfer types, including condos sold with a developer's public report, new construction with public offering statements, timeshares, leasehold-to-fee conversions, and family transfers.

questions.question_text: Under HRS 508D, which of the following is specifically excludable from mandatory seller disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: HIV status of a prior occupant
distractors: Known termite damage to the home
Active water intrusion in the basement
Presence of a cesspool on the property
questions.explanation: The AIDS/HIV status of any current or prior occupant is specifically excludable from mandatory disclosure. Facts with no physical effect on the structure are also excludable. Defects and hazards must still be disclosed.

questions.question_text: After the seller delivers the SRPDS, how many calendar days does the buyer have to review it?
questions.explanation: The buyer has 15 calendar days after receiving the disclosure to review it. During this period, the buyer may rescind the contract. The parties may modify these time periods by written agreement.

questions.question_text: Seller Akiko delivers the SRPDS on day 8 after acceptance. The buyer discovers undisclosed flood damage on day 20. Can the buyer still rescind?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the 15-day review period runs from delivery
questions.explanation: The 15-day review period starts when the buyer receives the disclosure, not from contract acceptance. Since Akiko delivered on day 8, the buyer's review period runs through day 23, so rescission on day 20 is within the window.

questions.question_text: Within how many calendar days of contract acceptance must the seller deliver the disclosure statement to the buyer?
questions.explanation: The seller must deliver the SRPDS within 10 calendar days of acceptance of the contract. After delivery, the buyer then has 15 calendar days to review and potentially rescind.

questions.question_text: A buyer who received the seller's disclosure statement wants to sue under HRS 508D for a disclosure violation. When must the action be commenced?
questions.correct_answer: Within 2 years of receiving the disclosure statement
distractors: Within 4 years of the date of transfer
Within 2 years of discovering the defect
Within 1 year of the closing date
questions.explanation: HRS 508D-17 requires any action under the chapter to commence within 2 years from the date the buyer received the disclosure statement. If no disclosure statement was ever delivered, the action must commence within 2 years of the recorded sale. This provision supersedes other laws on the time for commencing disclosure actions.

questions.question_text: If a seller fails to provide required disclosures under HRS 508D, the purchase contract becomes:
questions.explanation: When a seller fails to meet disclosure obligations, the contract is voidable at the buyer's option. This means the buyer can choose to rescind the contract or proceed despite the disclosure failure.

questions.question_text: A coastal Oahu property sits within the 3.2-foot sea level rise exposure area. What disclosure obligation does this create?
questions.correct_answer: Seller must disclose the exposure area status
distractors: Only disclose if buyer asks directly
questions.explanation: Under Act 179 (effective May 2022), sellers must disclose if a property is within the sea level rise exposure area (up to 3.2 feet of rise). Hawaii was the first state to require this environmental disclosure.

questions.question_text: A seller knows about volcanic smog (vog) regularly affecting air quality at the property. Must this be disclosed?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, as a material fact under HRS 508D
distractors: No, vog is not a disclosable condition
Only if the buyer has respiratory issues
Only for properties in Lava Zone 1
questions.explanation: While vog is not separately addressed by statute, it constitutes a material fact under HRS 508D because it would measurably affect the property's value to a reasonable person. All material facts must be disclosed regardless of whether they have a specific statutory provision.

questions.question_text: A home has a cesspool. What must the seller disclose about it beyond its mere existence?
questions.correct_answer: Its priority level and conversion deadline
distractors: Only the most recent inspection date
Only the cesspool's age and size
questions.explanation: Under Act 125 (2017), sellers must disclose not only that a cesspool exists but also its priority level for conversion and the statutory deadline (January 1, 2050). Starting January 2027, a standardized wastewater disclosure form will also be required.

questions.question_text: Starting January 1, 2027, sellers in Hawaii must complete which new form related to wastewater?
questions.explanation: HB 1749 requires a wastewater system disclosure form starting January 1, 2027. This is in addition to existing cesspool disclosure requirements under Act 125.

questions.question_text: A property on Oahu sits within a FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area and a tsunami inundation zone. How many of these environmental factors must the seller disclose?
questions.explanation: Both the SFHA (flood zone) designation and tsunami inundation zone status are material facts that must be disclosed under HRS 508D. Environmental hazards affecting value must all be disclosed to the buyer.
